coredata.dc:title: Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence detection of nitrite based on Microfluidic chip
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Jing
Wang, Xiong
Lin, Yitong
Zheng, Yongzan
Lin, Jin-Ming
coredata.dc:description: A chemiluminescent method for nitrite detection was developed on microfluidic chip. Carbon dots-NaNO2 − acidified H2O2 system was adopted. Chemiluminescence (CL) spectrum of this system was detected. The radiative recombination of hole-injected and electron-injected carbon dots explained their CL property. Spiral microchannels were designed on the microfluidic chip to allow enough reaction time for the carbon dots-NaNO2-acidified H2O2 system. Carbon dots and NaNO2 were premixed in the branch microchannel, then, the mixture reacted with acidified H2O2 in spiral microchannels. Concentrations of H2SO4 and H2O2, dilution ratio of carbon dots in H2O and flow rate were optimized to obtain the best CL signals. The approach presented satisfactory linear relationship between NaNO2 concentration and CL intensity. The tolerance of metal ions in determination of 1×10−5 M nitrite was analyzed. The nitrites in water and beverage samples were successfully analyzed on the microfluidic chip with good repeatability. The data were well accordance with the results obtained from GB 5009.33 − 2010. This microfluidic CL detection method is believed to be a simple, automatic and agent-save approach for inorganic ion analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic chip
Chemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Peroxynitrous acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the Microfluidic chip for CL analysis
Establishment of optimal conditions on microfluidic chip

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 21405143 , 21227006 , 81373373 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2-9-2014-023 ) and Innovation Program for Undergraduate in China University of Geosciences (Beijing) (No. 2015AX087 ). In the revised process, we thank for the help from Dr. Zhen Lin who works in Fujian Medical University and the funding from China Scholarship Council, China (CSC). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The design of the microfluidic chip to conduct peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL analysis.

CL kinetic study of the peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL system. (a) CL kinetic curves of the carbon dots -NaNO2-H2O2 system with different reagent mixing orders. (1): injecting carbon dots into H2O2 and NaNO2, (2): injecting NaNO2 into H2O2 and carbon dots, (3): injecting H2O2 into carbon dots and NaNO2.) (b) CL kinetic curves of the NaNO2-H2O2 system (1) and the carbon dots-H2O2 system (2). Experimental conditions: carbon dots with a dilution of 1:10, 100 μM NaNO2, 1M H2O2 in 0.005M H2SO4. All the injection volumes of the agents were 50μL. Voltage of the PMT was set at −13kV; the time interval was set as 0.1s.
CL spectrum (a) and mechanism (b) of the carbon dots-NaNO2-H2O2 system (Fig. 3b was reprinted with permission from ref. [19]. Copyright 2011 American Chemical Society.).

Condition optimization of the peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL system on the microfluidic chip. (a) Serial concentrations of H2SO4 were investigated. (b) Serial concentrations of H2O2 were investigated. (c) Different dilution ratios of CDs were investigated. (d) Flow rates were optimized. The standard error bars mean the variation of three individual experiments (n=6).

Microfluidic CL analysis for nitrite detection. (a) CL signals for the nitrite concentration in the range from 5 to 100μM. (b) Calibration curve of the microfluidic CL analysis method. The standard error bars mean the variation of three individual experiments (n=6). Experimental conditions: carbon dots in a dilution of 1:10, 1M H2O2 in 0.005M H2SO4, high voltage is −13kV, flow rate is 100μLmin−1.

Assessment of interference of metal ions on the determination of 1.0×10−5 M nitrite (Experimental conditions: additional concentrations of Na+, K+, Mg2+and Ca2+were 5.0×10−4 M; additional concentrations of Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+and Fe3+were 5.0×10−5 M; carbon dots in a dilution of 1:10; 1M H2O2 in 0.005M H2SO4; high voltage is −13kV; flow rate is 100μLmin−1). The standard error bars mean the variation of three individual experiments (n=6).

The nitrites in water and beverage samples detected by the microfluidic CL analysis method and the method in GB 5009.33-2010. (Microfluidic experiment conditions: carbon dots in a dilution of 1:10; 1M H2O2 in 0.005M H2SO4; high voltage is −13kV; flow rate is 100μLmin−1).

row.entry: Values detected by comparison method b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data were collected through three individual experiments (n=6).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values were detected by the method in GB 5009.33-2010.

head.ce:title: Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence detection of nitrite based on Microfluidic chip

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, China University of Geosciences (Beijing), Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Science, China University of Geosciences (Beijing)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, The Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, The Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Tsinghua University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A chemiluminescent method for nitrite detection was developed on microfluidic chip. Carbon dots-NaNO2 − acidified H2O2 system was adopted. Chemiluminescence (CL) spectrum of this system was detected. The radiative recombination of hole-injected and electron-injected carbon dots explained their CL property. Spiral microchannels were designed on the microfluidic chip to allow enough reaction time for the carbon dots-NaNO2-acidified H2O2 system. Carbon dots and NaNO2 were premixed in the branch microchannel, then, the mixture reacted with acidified H2O2 in spiral microchannels. Concentrations of H2SO4 and H2O2, dilution ratio of carbon dots in H2O and flow rate were optimized to obtain the best CL signals. The approach presented satisfactory linear relationship between NaNO2 concentration and CL intensity. The tolerance of metal ions in determination of 1×10−5 M nitrite was analyzed. The nitrites in water and beverage samples were successfully analyzed on the microfluidic chip with good repeatability. The data were well accordance with the results obtained from GB 5009.33 − 2010. This microfluidic CL detection method is believed to be a simple, automatic and agent-save approach for inorganic ion analysis.
A microfluidic chemiluminescence detection method was developed to analyze nitrites in water samples by adopting Carbon dots (CDs)-NaNO2-acidified H2O2 system.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrites widely exist in nature and are involved in the overall nitrogen cycle [1]. Nitrites are used as fertilizing reagents and preservatives in food processing. The nitrogen-based fertilizers are regarded as important contamination sources in the environment [2]. Nitrites absorbed from daily food play a key physiological role in human body [3]. They can react with secondary or tertiary amines to form carcinogenic N-nitrosamine which is harmful to human beings [4–7]. At the presence of nitrites, hemoglobin changed to be methaemoglobin impairing the ability of the blood to transport oxygen throughout the body [8]. Therefore, nitrite detection is significant to the environment and human health.

Several methods have been reported to detect nitrite [9–12]. A turn-on fluorogenic and chromogenic probe was devised to selectively respond to nitrite ion in water based on diazotization reaction. The color of solution was changed from colorless to orange by the formation of rhodamine B via an analyte triggered fragmentation reaction [13]. Pagliano et. al [14] applied quadruple isotope dilution mass spectrometry to simultaneously determine nitrite and nitrate in seawater. Hemoglobin was modified on glassy carbon electrode to construct an electrochemical biosensor for hydrogen peroxide and nitrite sensing [15]. Writing sensor was even fabricated to enable in situ detection of nitrite with graphite and silver powder as electrode inks [16]. However, these analytical methods suffer from some disadvantages that hard operation, laborious fabrication or request of expensive equipments. As an attractive analytical approach, chemiluminescence (CL) shows some prominent advantages, such as low-cost instrument, high sensitivity, easy operation and the lack of interference from background [17,18]. In previous work, we reported peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL of fluorescent carbon dots for nitrite sensing [19]. Flow injection was utilized to successfully determine nitrites in water samples while the agent consumption was not small.

Microfluidic technology shows great advantages in analytical chemistry, such as rapid analysis, small agent consumption, miniaturization, easy control and operation [20–22]. With these tremendous advances, microfluidics could integrate technologies in various fields to perform quick and reproducible analysis of samples with small volumes as well as eliminate the labor-intensive and potentially error-prone laboratory manipulations. Almost every conventional analytical tool in analytical chemistry labs has an equivalent microfabricated counterpart on chips. CL detection also is considered to be one of the suitable detection methods that best matches the microfluidic analysis system. Lapa et. al who devised a multi-pumping flow system for chemiluminometric determination of carvedilol [23] integrated complementary metal oxide semiconductor array detectors (CMOSADs) of different brands on microfluidic device for CL detection and applied them to determine nitrite in ground water samples [24]. The integration of microfluidic device with CMOSADs achieved the goal that building a CL detector with low financial resources and gave an inspiration for us to further develop chemiluminometric determination of nitrite on microfluidic platform.

In order to save agent and further improve the automation of the detection system, we provided a peroxynitrous acid (ONOOH)-induced chemiluminescent method for nitrite detection based on microfluidic chip. Nitrite can react with H2O2 to form online ONOOH which make carbon dots to be chemiluminescent. Several experimental conditions were optimized to obtain high sensitivity, such as flow rate, the dilution ratio of carbon dots, H2SO4 and H2O2 concentration. The CL intensity was enhanced linearly with the increase of nitrite concentration. The validation of this method was demonstrated by GB 5009.33-2010 and several samples were successfully analyzed on the microfluidic chip we devised.

Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) Sylgard 184 and its curing agent were bought from Dow Corning (Midland, MI, USA). Negative photoresist SU-8 2050 (Microchem, Newton, MA, USA) was used to fabricate the microchannels on the microfluidic chip. Silicon wafers were obtained from Xilika Crystal polishing Material Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Glass slides were obtained from Sigma Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). L-serine and polyethylene glycol 1500 (PEG 1500) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Co. (St. Louis, MO, USA). Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%) and glycerine were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. (Beijing, China). Sodium nitrite (NaNO2) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 35%) and quinine sulfate were bought from Alfa Aesar China, Ltd. (Beijing, China). Purified water (>18MΩcm) was used throughout the work. All the reagents were of analytical-grade and used as received without further purification.

Negative photoresist SU-8 2050 was coated onto a silicon wafer using a spin coater (Institute of Microelectronics of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China). A model JKG-2A photolithography machine (Xueze Optical Machine Co., Ltd. Shanghai, China) was used to expose and pattern the microchannels on the mold under the protection of the designed photomask. The PDMS replicas were sealed with glass slide by a model PDC-32g oxygen plasma (Harrick Plasma, Ithaca, NY, USA). CL experiment was performed on a BPCL ultraweak CL analyzer (Institute of Biophysics of Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing, China). A model Harvard Apparatus PHD 2000 syringe pump (Instech Laboratories, Inc., Holliston, MA, USA) was used to inject the solutions into the microchannels on the microfluidic chip. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image was collected using a Model JEM 2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Model UV-3900 spectraphotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectrum collection was performed on a Model FL-7000 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Model 100 FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA).

Standard soft photolithography and replica molding techniques were used to fabricate the spiral microchannels on the chip. Briefly, negative photoresist SU-8 2050 was spun onto the silicon wafer at a speed of 1000rpm for 60s using a spin-coater. After baking at 65°C for 20min, UV light exposure and development were done to generate the mold of microchannels. The width and depth of the microchannels are 0.5mm and 30µm, respectively. The cycle number of the microchannels is four. PDMS prepolymer and curing agent were premixed as 10:1 (by mass) and poured onto the molds. After degassed under vacuum for 0.5h, the mixture was put into an oven for curing at 75°C for 2h. The PDMS was peeled off carefully and cut into the rectangular shape (length: 5cm, width: 3cm, height: 5mm). Inlets and outlets of microchannels were made by a flat-tipped syringe needle. The PDMS replica and glass slide were sealed together via oxygen plasma (PDC-32g, Harrick Plasma, Ithaca, NY, USA) treatment for 90s.

Fluorescent carbon dots were used in this experiment and their preparation process was the same as the description in ref. [19]. 1.0g of PEG 1500 was added into 15mL of glycerine and the mixture was treated by microwave to become a homogeneous solution. Then, 1.0g L-serine was added. The mixture was further heated for 10min in the microwave oven. The solution changed to be brown and was purified by dialysis using the dialysis membrane with cutoff M w≈2000.The carbon dots solution was collected in a tube and diluted by water before use in CL experiment. CL kinetic curves were collected in the quartz cuvette through batch experiments to investigate the optimum addition orders of the reagents. The CL profiles were displayed and integrated at intervals of 0.1s. Voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 1.2kV. Carbon dots (a dilution of 1:10, 50μL) and NaNO2 (100 μM, 50μL) were premixed, and then H2O2 (1M, 50μL) in H2SO4 (0.005M) was injected by a microliter syringe from the upper injection port. CL spectrum was measured on the BPCL ultraweak CL analyzer with high-energy cutoff filters from 400 to 640nm between the microfluidic chip and PMT as described in Ref. [25]. The addition orders of the reagents were changed to get the highest CL signals and decide the flow injection order on the microfluidic chip. There are three inlets on the microfluidic chip. NaNO2, carbon dots and H2O2 in H2SO4 were pumped into the microchannels by a syringe pump. Carbon dots and NaNO2 solution (100μM) were premixed in the branch microchannel, then, they were mixed with acidified H2O2 in the spiral microchannels. Different H2SO4 concentrations (0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.10M), H2O2 concentrations (0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1 and 5M), dilution ratios of carbon dots (1:5, 1:10, 1:40, 1:100, 1:200 and 1:400) and flow rates (50, 100, 150, 200 and 300μLmin−1) were tested to obtain the best experimental conditions. The univariate method is used in systematically optimizing experimental parameters by varying one parameter at a time while keeping the rest constant. Calibration curve was obtained by detect the CL intensity versus different NaNO2 concentrations. The tolerance of metal ions on detection of 1×10−5 M nitrite was investigated with carbon dots in a dilution of 1:10 and 1MH2O2 in 0.005MH2SO4. High voltage was set at −13kV and flow rate was 100μLmin−1. The additional concentration of Na+, K+, Mg2+and Ca2+was 5.0×10−4 M while that of Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+and Fe3+ was 5.0×10−5 M. Some water and beverage samples were analyzed under the optimized conditions on the microfluidic chip. Freshly collected samples were filtered through a membrane filter with 0.22µm pore size and spiked with 1.0×10−5 M EDTA to mask transition metals before CL analysis. Boiled water was cooled before any treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the Microfluidic chip for CL analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Spiral microchannels were designed on the microfluidic chip to give enough reaction time for the peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL system ( Fig. 1). NaNO2 and carbon dots were mixed in advance. Then, the mixture reacted with acidified H2O2 to give the CL emission. Microfluidic chip was chosen as the platform for CL analysis based on the following reasons: (1) Agents were pumped into the microchannels automatically and the results were read out directly. The automatic process makes the analytical operation to be easy and quick. No more than 1min was needed for one test. (2) Agent consumption was decreased sharply. The width of the microchannels was 500µm. There were four circles of microchannels on the chip so only ~1μL agent was needed once a time. (3) The good compatibility of the microfluidic chip with the CL analyzer. The microfluidic chip made of PDMS and glass substrate presented a prominent transparency for luminescence, so that the CL detection could be performed on it. Despite this, the microfluidic chip was conveniently installed in front of the PMT without any modification of the CL analyzer.

Various adding orders of reagents were tested to decide the flow injection order on the microfluidic chip. As shown in Fig. 2a, the highest CL signals were obtained through adding acidified H2O2 into the mixture of NaNO2 and carbon dots. Neither the injection of carbon dots into H2O2 and NaNO2 nor the injection of NaNO2 into H2O2 and carbon dots could get the best CL signals. The transform of ONOOH to nitrate via the stage of HOONO* is believed to be one step generating light emission [26,27]. ONOOH is the product of reaction of acidified H2O2 and NaNO2 [28,29]. With the addition of acidified H2O2, the UV–vis absorption peak of NaNO2 decreased and another peak located at 301nm appeared due to the isomerization of ONOOH (Fig. S1a). There was no reaction before injecting H2O2 into the mixture of NaNO2 and carbon dots. ONOOH adequately reacted with carbon dots then enhanced the CL intensity. Mixing of NaNO2 with acidified H2O2 without carbon dots also generated CL while the signals were rapid and weak (curve 1 in Fig. 2b). Weak and lasting CL emission was observed from the reaction between carbon dots and H2O2 indicating the minor contribution of H2O2 to the highest CL (curve 2 in Fig. 2b). All the kinetic curves demonstrated that the reactions between ONOOH related species and carbon dots were the main pathway for the CL. According to these results, NaNO2 and carbon dots were premixed in the branch microchannels. Then, the mixture reacted with acidified H2O2 in spiral microchannels to generate CL emission.

The carbon dots used in this work were spherical dots with the average diameter of ~3–4nm (Fig. S1b). The wide range of 450–600nm in the CL spectrum for carbon dots-NaNO2-H2O2 system is similar to the fluorescent emission wavelength of the carbon dots ( Fig. 3a and S1c). Hence, the various surface energy traps existing on the carbon dots could reasonably explain the CL [30–32]. FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. S1d) indicated C-OH,-CH3,-CH2, C-O-C and C-N groups were formed on the carbon dots. CL mechanism and the role of carbon dots playing in this system are clearly demonstrated in Fig. 3b and equation 1-10. Singlet oxygen (1O2) which is reported to be CL emitter in the NO2 −-H2O2 system is generated through reaction 1–5, while it is not the main CL emitting species responsible for the increased CL in the carbon dots-NaNO2-H2O2 system [19,33]. Carbon dots are proved to be the main emitters, rather than catalysts in the CL system. Radiative electron-hole annihilation between hole-injected and electron-injected carbon dots (R·+and R·−) is the main cause leading to the CL emission [34–37]. Oxidant, ONOOH and OH·which is generated in the mixing process of nitrites and acidified H2O2 inject hole into carbon dots and covert carbon dots to be R·+(reaction 6 and 7) [38]. O2 ·-that is produced in the reaction of ONOOH and acidified H2O2 donates electron to carbon dots and makes carbon dots to be R·-(reaction 8) [33,36,39]. Electron-hole annihilation in or between the R·+and R·-resulted in the energy release associating with CL emission (reaction 9 and 10) [19].
(1) HNO2+H2O2→ONOOH + H2O
(2) ONOOH → ONOOH*→NO3 -+H+
(3) ONOOH →·NO2+·OH
(4) ONOOH + H2O2→O2 ·-+·NO2+H++H2O
(5) O2 ·-+·OH → 1O2+OH−
(6) R+ONOOH + H+→R·++·NO2+H2O
(7) R+·OH + H+→R·++·H2O
(8) R+O2 ·-→R·-+O2
(9) R·++R·-→R*+R
(10) R*→R+hv a

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of optimal conditions on microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the conditions for the analysis of nitrites on microfluidic chip, serial concentrations of H2SO4, H2O2, carbon dots and flow rates were investigated. No CL signals could be observed at the absence of H2SO4 demonstrating acidification was indispensable in this system ( Fig. 4a). The reason was that reactive substance ONOOH should be formed in an acidic medium. The best CL signal was obtained with the H2SO4 concentration of 0.005M. CL signals increased with the increasing of H2O2 concentrations (Fig. 4b). In order to save the H2O2 consumption, 1M H2O2 was chosen in the following analysis and brought no obvious influence on the feasibility of the method. As shown in Fig. 4c, carbon dots with a dilution of 1:10 provided the highest CL emission. For the microfluidic analysis system, flow rate is an influential factor. 100μLmin−1 was selected as the suitable flow rate in this work although the CL signal increased with the flow rates in the range from 50 to 300μLmin−1 (Fig. 4d). Too high flow rates increase the pressure in microchannels then lead to the leakage of the chip. The passing time of reagent in spiral microchannels decreases at the too high flow rate results in the reduced CL signals. Too low flow rates generate a broad and weak CL signal. Therefore, the optimal analytical conditions on microfluidic chip are 1MH2O2 in 0.005M H2SO4, a dilution of 1:10 carbon dots and flow rate of 100μLmin−1.

Calibration curve was collected under the optimal conditions. The linearity of the CL analysis method for nitrite detection based on microfluidic chip was investigated with serial concentrations of NaNO2 ranging from 5 to 100μM. CL intensity increased linearly with the rising of nitrite concentration ( Fig. 5). There was a good linear relationship between CL intensity and the nitrite concentration (y=9.044x+46.75, R 2=0.9973). This approach presented satisfactory reproducibility and RSD values (n=6) of the analysis were 3.5%, 0.5% and 0.3% for nitrite concentrations of 5, 50 and 100μM, respectively. The microfluidic CL analysis method exhibited great potential and capability for quantitative detection of nitrite in samples. The interference of metal ions on the CL detection was investigated. The tolerance limit was estimated with a ±5% relative error in peak height. Most metal ions had no interferences on the CL intensity which exhibited no obvious change after metal ion addition ( Fig. 6). The tolerance concentration of Na+, K+, Mg2+and Ca2+was <5.0×10−4 M while that of Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+and Fe3+ was <5.0×10−5 M.

The proposed method was successfully applied in determination of the nitrites in water and beverage samples. As shown in Table 1, the nitrite concentration in the five water samples ranked in the order of pond water >boiled water>tap water>mineral water>spring water. Five beverage samples also were analyzed on the microfluidic chip. The validation of the proposed method was evaluated by a national food safety standard (GB 5009.33-2010). The results obtained with the two methods were in good agreement demonstrating the good reliability of the method we provided. All the RSDs were below 10% demonstrating the good repeatability.

In summary, we developed a microfluidic nitrite detection method based on peroxynitrous-acid-induced CL in the presence of carbon dots. Experimental conditions were optimized to obtain the best results. Nitrites in water and beverage samples were successfully analyzed using this method with good reliability and reproducibility. Compared to the flow injection CL method, the microfluidic one possesses advantages that high automation, simple operation and small reagent consumption. In future work, various microfluidic chips could be designed according to the requests of CL detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Nos. 21405143, 21227006, 81373373), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2-9-2014-023) and Innovation Program for Undergraduate in China University of Geosciences (Beijing) (No. 2015AX087). In the revised process, we thank for the help from Dr. Zhen Lin who works in Fujian Medical University and the funding from China Scholarship Council, China (CSC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2016.03.062.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Utilizing of Ag@AgCl@graphene oxide@Fe3O4 nanocomposite as a magnetic plasmonic nanophotocatalyst in light-initiated H2O2 generation and chemiluminescence detection of nitrite

Modifying a known gelator scaffold for nitrite detection

Microfluidic paper-based analytical device for the determination of nitrite and nitrate
A reaction based turn-on type fluorogenic and chromogenic probe for the detection of trace amount of nitrite in water
High-precision quadruple isotope dilution method for simultaneous determination of nitrite and nitrate in seawater by GCMS after derivatization with triethyloxonium tetrafluoroborate
Enhancing sensitivity of hemoglobin-based electrochemical biosensor by using protein conformational intermediate
Writing sensors on solid agricultural products for in situ detection
Application of chemiluminescence in the analysis of wastewaters – a review
Detection of trace nitrite in waters using a QDs-based chemiluminescence analysis system
Peroxynitrous-acid-induced chemiluminescence of fluorescent carbon dots for nitrite sensing
Latest developments in micro total analysis systems
Latest developments in microfluidic cell biology and analysis systems
Chemiluminometric determination of carvedilol in a multi-pumping flow system
CMOS arrays as chemiluminescence detectors on microfluidic devices
Electrochemiluminescence of luminol in alkaline solution at a Paraffin-Impregnated graphite electrode

Radical and concerted mechanisms in oxidations of amines, sulfides, and alkenes by peroxynitrite, peroxynitrous acid, and the peroxynitrite−CO2 adduct: density functional theory transition structures and energetics
Investigation of the interaction of sodium nitrite with hydrogen peroxide in aqueous solutions by the chemiluminescence method
Interaction of nitrous acid with hydrogen peroxide and with water
Flow-injection chemiluminescent determination of nitrite in water based on the formation of peroxynitrite from the reaction of nitrite and hydrogen peroxide
Commercially activated carbon as the source for producing multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by chemical oxidation
Carbon dots for optical imaging in vivo
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Reaction between peroxynitrite and hydrogen-peroxide - formation of oxygen and slowing of peroxynitrite decomposition
Extraction of electrochemiluminescent oxidized carbon quantum dots from activated carbon
Electrochemiluminescence of water-soluble carbon nanocrystals released electrochemically from graphite
Electrochemistry and electrogenerated chemiluminescence from silicon nanocrystal quantum dots
Molecular dynamics simulation of the HOONO decomposition and the HO(center dot)/NO(2)(center dot) caged radical pair in water
Observation of pH-, solvent-, spin-, and excitation-dependent blue photoluminescence from carbon nanoparticles
